title: One-vs-One (OVO) Multiclass
concept: One-vs-One (OVO) solves multi-class classification by training a separate binary model for every possible pair of classes.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a 4-class problem, how does One-vs-Rest (OVR) decide the final prediction?

options.text: It runs all 4 classifiers and selects the class with the highest probability score
It compares all classes in pairs and runs a tournament bracket
It sums all probabilities and divides by 4 to get an average

exercises.explain: Correct! OVR runs all class-specific models and picks the one with the highest output score. Now, let's explore an alternative multi-class method.

cards.title: Sports tournament
cards.body: In a round-robin league, every team plays every other team exactly once. To find the overall winner among Team A, B, and C, we match A vs B, B vs C, and A vs C.

cards.title: One-vs-One, defined
cards.body: One-vs-One (OVO) is a multi-class method that trains a binary classifier for every possible pair of classes. Each model ignores all other classes except the two it is comparing.

cards.title: Pairwise voting
cards.body: Imagine voting for your favorite fruit among Apple, Banana, and Cherry. Instead of rating them individually, you are asked: Apple vs Banana? Banana vs Cherry? Apple vs Cherry? The fruit with the most votes wins.

cards.title: Tallying the votes
cards.body: For [Cat, Dog, Bird], OVO trains 3 pairs: 1) Cat vs Dog, 2) Dog vs Bird, 3) Cat vs Bird. If Cat beats Dog, Dog beats Bird, and Cat beats Bird, Cat has 2 votes, Dog 1, Bird 0. Cat wins!

cards.title: Myth: OVO is always faster
cards.body: While each pair's dataset is smaller, you need many more models! For 10 classes, OVR needs 10 models, but OVO needs 45 models ($10 \times 9 / 2$).

cards.title: OVR vs OVO comparison
cards.body: Two different strategies to solve multi-class classification using simple yes/no models:

left.text:
Trains N models
Each model compares 1 class vs all others combined

right.text:
Trains N*(N-1)/2 models
Each model compares a pair of classes directly

exercises.prompt: How many binary classifiers does One-vs-One (OVO) train for a 4-class classification problem?
exercises.explain: For 4 classes, OVO trains $4 \times (4 - 1) / 2 = 6$ classifiers: (A vs B, A vs C, A vs D, B vs C, B vs D, C vs D).

exercises.prompt: True or false: An OVO classifier comparing Cat vs Dog is trained on the entire dataset, including images of Birds.
exercises.explain: False! The Cat vs Dog model ignores all other classes, meaning it is only trained on Cat and Dog data, making each individual training set smaller.

exercises.sentence: In OVO, the final class is selected using a ___ system, where the class that wins the most pairwise matchups is chosen.
exercises.explain: Each binary classifier casts a vote for its winner. The class with the most votes across all pairings is the final prediction.

exercises.prompt: Which system is using a One-vs-One (OVO) classification strategy?

options.text: A system that trains [Red vs Blue], [Blue vs Green], and [Red vs Green] models
A system that trains [Red vs not-Red], [Blue vs not-Blue], and [Green vs not-Green] models
A system that trains a single model to predict color codes from 0 to 255

exercises.explain: OVO focuses exclusively on pairwise comparisons, matching every class against every other class individually.

exercises.prompt: Why might OVO be preferred over OVR for algorithms that do not scale well with large datasets?

options.text: Each pairwise classifier is trained on a much smaller subset of the data
OVO trains fewer total models than OVR
OVO completely eliminates the need for decision thresholds
OVO only works on linear boundaries

exercises.explain: Since each classifier only looks at data from two classes, the training set size is much smaller, which helps algorithms that scale poorly on large datasets.

exercises.prompt: You have a 3-class problem [Apple, Banana, Cherry]. The matchups output: Apple beats Banana, Cherry beats Apple, and Cherry beats Banana. Which class wins?

options.text: It's a tie between Apple and Cherry

exercises.explain: Spot on! Cherry won against both Apple and Banana, giving it 2 votes. Apple won 1 (against Banana), and Banana won 0. Cherry is the clear winner! You've mastered One-vs-One multiclass!
